topic.what: Conflict between RTOS timekeeping and atomic trusted computing services on ARM TrustZone-M microcontrollers causing SysTick suppression and time drift
topic.how: Secure-driven time synchronization where Secure World intercepts SysTick events and reconstructs kernel state after atomic service completion without RTOS modifications
topic.why_matters: Real-time applications depend on accurate tick-based scheduling; security services require atomic execution; their fundamental conflict causes time drift that can jeopardize safety-critical embedded systems

characteristics.what_it_measures: CPU cycles to handle intercepted SysTick in Secure World
Latency per SysTick event when dedicated Secure SysTick unit present
characteristics.context: Per cross-world transition on platforms with banked SysTick

characteristics.evidence: Section 6: drift eliminated across all tested configurations
characteristics.context: No observable long-term divergence with mechanism enabled

characteristics.what_itasures: Maximum time drift per hot-patching invocation
characteristics.context: 128KB binary install; both FreeRTOS and Zephyr affected

characteristics.what_itasures: Maximum time drift per attestation invocation
Linear drift increment rate for CFI instrumented applications
characteristics.evidence: Figure 2: each application exhibits distinct linear drift rate
characteristics.context: Depends on branch instrumentation density per workload

characteristics.what_it_measures: Lines of Secure World code added
Speed RMSE for PID motor control with uncompensated drift
characteristics.context: Post-transient mean error grows 1.07 to 4.11 rad/s

characteristics.what_it_measures: Position RMSE for servo with single tick freeze
characteristics.evidence: Figure 6: single 190-tick freeze causes missed controller release

good_for: TrustZone-M microcontrollers running RTOS with tick-based scheduling
Control systems requiring precise real-time guarantees with security services
Deployments using FreeRTOS or Zephyr on ARM Cortex-M33
Systems where security services require atomic execution (cryptography, attestation, hot-patching)
not_for: Non-TrustZone platforms or interrupt-driven systems without tick counter
Systems with very high frequency secure service invocations (>1kHz)
Complex RTOS kernels requiring extensive state reconstruction beyond available kernel primitives
Single-tick atomic windows with negligible drift impact
requires: Tick-driven RTOS with accessible kernel structures
Compile-time knowledge of Non-Secure kernel data layout
Minimal modifications to Secure World only
